coredata.dc:title: A novel turn-on fluorescence sensor based on the Nd (III) complex for the ultrasensitive detection of 6-mercaptopurine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Rajpal,
Jana, Subhajit
Ojha, Ravi Prakash
Prakash, Rajiv
coredata.dc:description: 6-mercaptopurine (6MP) is a chemotherapeuticdrug widely used for treating inflammatory bowel diseases and several cancers. Nevertheless, determining and monitoring its concentration in the human body is highly important because over or under-doses of 6MP can lead to critical health issues. In this paper, we have developed a turn-on fluorescent probe for the determination of the anticancer drug 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) based on coordination complex [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3]. [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] has been synthesized through a simple precipitation process taking the stoichiometric ratio of Nd (III) nitrate hexahydrate and 2-aminobenzoic acid (2-ABA), commonly known as anthranilic acid (Anth). The synthesis and structure have been investigated and validated by different characterizations like UV–visible spectroscopy, FT-IR, HRMS, XPS, and SEM. The synthesized complex displayed excellent fluorescence properties, and the fluorescence intensity was enhanced with the addition of 6MP in the form of a [Fe (6MP)3]2+ mixed complex (Fe-6MP), which is formed by dissolving it in FeCl3. The fabricated sensors displayed the best linear response in a wide range of concentrations from 2.55 μM to 45.51 μM of 6MP. The lower limit of detection (LOD) of the developed sensor was found to be 0.26 μM with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99. The synthesized probe gives an acceptable response for the sensing of 6MP in the presence of several interfering agents.

EDS investigation of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex. (a) SEM image of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex used for elemental mapping, (b) EDS spectrum. Elemental mapping shows the distribution of (c) Nd, (d) C, (e) O, and (f) N.
XPS study of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex. (a) XPS survey spectrum, Deconvoluted high-resolution spectra of (b) Nd 3d, (c) N 1s, (d) C 1s, and (e) O 1s.

Investigation of fluorescence properties of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex. (a) Excitation spectrum recorded for emission wavelength fixed at 403 nm, (b) Emission spectrum recorded for a fixed excitation of 354 nm, (c) Emission spectrum showing comparative study of PL properties of Nd-Anth, Fe-6MP, and [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex, (d) Bar diagram representing pH optimization. For recording the spectra, 2 ml solution (concentration: 50 μg/mL, pH = 7.4) of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex, 6-MP (45.5 μM) in 15 μM FeCl3 aqueous solution, 0.1 M phosphate buffer were used.

(a) Fluorescence decay curve for [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex and [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex + Fe-6MP (1:1), and [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex + Fe-6MP (1:2); (b) emission spectra of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex and excitation spectra of Fe-6MP. head.ce:title: A novel turn-on fluorescence sensor based on the Nd (III) complex for the ultrasensitive detection of 6-mercaptopurine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University), Varanasi, India

Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors have contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 6-mercaptopurine (6MP) is a chemotherapeuticdrug widely used for treating inflammatory bowel diseases and several cancers. Nevertheless, determining and monitoring its concentration in the human body is highly important because over or under-doses of 6MP can lead to critical health issues. In this paper, we have developed a turn-on fluorescent probe for the determination of the anticancer drug 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) based on coordination complex [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3]. [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] has been synthesized through a simple precipitation process taking the stoichiometric ratio of Nd (III) nitrate hexahydrate and 2-aminobenzoic acid (2-ABA), commonly known as anthranilic acid (Anth). The synthesis and structure have been investigated and validated by different characterizations like UV–visible spectroscopy, FT-IR, HRMS, XPS, and SEM. The synthesized complex displayed excellent fluorescence properties, and the fluorescence intensity was enhanced with the addition of 6MP in the form of a [Fe (6MP)3]2+ mixed complex (Fe-6MP), which is formed by dissolving it in FeCl3. The fabricated sensors displayed the best linear response in a wide range of concentrations from 2.55 μM to 45.51 μM of 6MP. The lower limit of detection (LOD) of the developed sensor was found to be 0.26 μM with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99. The synthesized probe gives an acceptable response for the sensing of 6MP in the presence of several interfering agents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, there are many types of lifesaving drugs, such as docetaxel, oxaliplatin, and cisplatin, which are commonly used for breast cancer, colon cancer, and lung cancer, respectively [1–3]. However, if their amount exceeds a certain level, they can induce toxicity [4]. 6-Mercaptopurine (6MP) is similar to nucleobase (purine base) and is also an immunosuppressive, chemotherapeutic, anticancer drug [4–6]. It is a curatively important antineoplastic and antimetabolite drug, broadly used in treating several diseases, including multiple myeloma, human leukemia, chorionic epithelial cell carcinoma, systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, malignant lymphoma, [7–9]. It is also used in inflammatory bowel diseases (such as ulcerative colitis and Crohn’s disease), the rejection of organ transplantation, and anticancer chemotherapy for trade serious lymphoblastic leukemia [4,8,10,11]. Since 6MP is quickly removed through the renal system [8,10], the amount of 6MP in human liquid is crucial; either higher or lower doses of this medicine can lead to critical health problems [5]. 6MP has a fine therapeutic indicator, and the under-dose rise in 6MP may cause poisonousness [6]. Accordingly, the higher dosage of 6MP might cause bone marrow destruction and liver problems [6,12,13]. Hence, the determination of the accurate concentration of 6MP is crucial in the human body.

There are several analytical tools and methods that have been utilized for the detection of 6MP, such as Raman spectroscopy [14], Mass spectroscopy [15], capillary electrophoresis [16], ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (using the colorimetric technique) [17,18], electrochemical methods [19,20], chemiluminescence [21–23], fluorescence spectroscopy method [17,24,25] and also including the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method [25,26]. Among these methods, the electrochemical processes are commonly used, but owing to their poor repeatability and constraints in electrode adjustment, they find limited application in real samples [7,9]. Similarly, in the case of HPLC-based sensing, although it is a selective and sensitive technique, it involves the application of expensive equipment and ultrapure poisonous solvents, which limits their application [6,7,9]. The capillary electrode and Raman spectroscopy techniques also require exclusive and sophisticated instruments in 6MP sensing [27]. Hence, creating a selective and sensitive technique for the sensing of 6MP is necessary. Alternatively, the fluorescent spectroscopic method offers a simple, rapid, low-cost technique for the sensing of 6MP. Our study exhibited that fluorescence sensors emerge as very acceptable as a result of their rapid response, facile synthesis, and high sensitivity.

The coordination number of Nd (III) ions is recognized to differ from 8 to 10, reliant on the nature of ligands, which supports several geometries [28–30]. The fluorescence emission intensity of Nd3+ ions in great vibrational frequency mediums, due to the 4F3/2 →4I11/2 transition, depends on the selection of solvents [29,31,32]. Now, there are mainly three conditions for the fluorescence: (1) destruction of vibrational excitation; (2) prevention of radiation-less cross-relaxation throughout the collisions; and (3) repression of solvent assimilation in the producing Nd (III) ions [29]. Nd (III) generally forms complexes with beta-diketones (such as acetylacetone, dibenzoyl methane, etc.), 2-ABA, Diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid (DTPA), chelating ligands due to its high electropositive nature [28,29,33–35]. Anthranilic acid (Anth) is a significant precursor to tryptophan, one of the geologically significant alpha-amino acids that also appear stable, extremely insoluble complexes with a variation of bivalent and trivalent metal ions [36]. Here, we have selected the 2-ABA (Anth) as the π-rich conjugated ligand that enhances the emission intensity. The aromatic ligand tends to form a hydrogen bond and exhibits π–π stacking interaction with the analyte [37,38]. As such, rare earth (or Lanthanide) metals are commonly used for the formation of fluorescent rare earth metal–ligand coordination complexes [37–39].

In the present work, we have developed the fluorescent [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex with the help of 2-ABA and Nd (III) nitrate hexahydrate. After initial confirmation from the high-resolution mass spectrum (HR-MS) data, as shown in Fig. S2, this fluorescent coordination complex is further characterized by Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) for its structural and morphological analysis. In the [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex, the Nd3+ ion is surrounded by a bidentate 2-ABA ligand and three labile water molecules. The synthesized Nd (III) complex has shown excellent fluorescence properties, which are further enhanced by the addition of 6MP in the form of [Fe (6MP)3]2+. The mixed complex, Fe-6MP, has been formed by simply dissolving 6MP in FeCl3. The enhancement in signal may be due to the π–π stacking interaction and formation of hydrogen bonds of the ligand with the Fe-6MP. The fabricated sensor exhibited the best linear response in a large range of concentration from 2.55 μM to 45.51 μM with a detection limit of 0.26 μM and a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99.

Neodymium (III) nitrate hexahydrate [Nd (NO3)3·6H2O] (99.9 %), L-glutathione (C10H17N3O6S) (98 %). Sodium phosphate dibasic [Na2HPO4] (99 %), Sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate [NaHPO4·2H2O] (99 %), Bovine serum albumin (95 %), and Creatine hydrate [C4H9N3O2·H2O] (99 %) from Sigma-Aldrich, D-(+)-glucose anhydrous (C6H12O6) (99.5 %), and L-lysine (C6H14N2O2) (99.9 %) were obtained from HIMEDA, India. Glycine [H2NCH2COOH] (99.5 %), Sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH) (97 %), 6-Mercaptopurine monohydrate [C5H4N4S·H2O] (98 %), Calcium chloride dihydrate [CaCl2·2H2O] (97 %) and NaCl (99 %) were gotten from Merck, India. L-ascorbic acid (vitamin C) [C6H18O6] (99.7 %), Ferric chloride anhydrous [FeCl3] (97 %), and Potassium sulphate [K2SO4] (99.5 %) were purchased from SDFCL, India. L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S) (99 %), Uric acid [C5H4N4O3] (99 %), and Galactose (C6H12O6) (99.8 %) were purchased from SRL, India. 2-amino benzoic acid (anthranilic acid) [C7H7NO2] (>98 %) was obtained from Rolex, India. Here, all chemical compounds were employed without any further purification.

UV–vis spectra were performed for liquid complex and solid complex using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600) SHIMADZU. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using an aqueous solution of the Neodymium-anthranilic acid [(Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex on F-4600 FL Spectrophotometers and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra recorded using alpha 300, WITECH. Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed on Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS5 instrument from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was recorded on Thermo Fischer Scientific ESCALAB Xi+, and Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken on EVO- scanning electron microscope MA 15/18 instrument.

The synthesis of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex (as shown in Fig. S1) was carried out according to the previously reported simple precipitation method [28,40]. Briefly, in this process, at first, 0.05 M (225 mg) of Nd (NO3)3·6H2O was solubilized in 5 ml double distilled water. In another round bottom flask, 0.15 M (209 mg) of anthranilic acid (Anth) were solubilized in 5 ml ethanol (CH3CH2OH), that was gradually added in the aqueous solution of Nd (NO3)3·6H2O at continuous stirring mode, and 75 °C temperature. The pH of the whole reaction combination is maintained by the dropwise mixing of sodium hydroxide solution. The appearance of precipitation was observed during the mixing of NaOH solution in the whole mixture, and at a solution pH of ∼5, and then a faint permanent precipitate formed. The whole reaction mixture was left at 75 °C temperature under continuous stirring for 12 h. Then, the reaction combination was cooled at room temperature, and the precipitate was filtered off. The as-synthesized [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex was cleaned thoroughly with double distilled water and then ethyl alcohol and finally dried at 80 °C under vacuum. The synthesis was confirmed initially through the high-resolution mass spectrum (HR-MS) as shown in the supplementary information (SI).

The initial optical and structural investigations of the synthesized [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex were done by UV–vis and FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 1 (a) displays the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of both ligand Anth and its complex with Nd (III). The result shows that the absorption cut edge for the ligand is located at 383 nm, and after complexation with Nd, the absorption edge shifted ∼3 nm towards a higher wavelength value (386 nm), i.e., the bandgap decreases, which is expected as the conjugation length is increased in the complex. Several peaks are visible in the spectrum of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3], which correspond to several electronic transitions from the ground state, 4I9/2, to higher excited states [41,42] of Nd3+, as pointed out in the graph. This study confirms the presence of Nd3+ ions in the as-synthesized complex.

In order to further explore the functionalities, FTIR spectra of both Anth and [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex were recorded as shown in Fig. 1 (b). Excluding a few deviations in stretching frequencies (generally decreasing in stretching frequency), both exhibitions have nearly similar spectra. In the spectrum of Anth, there are two absorption peaks that appear at 3475 cm−1 and 3322 cm−1, which ascribes the NH asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration, respectively, of an amino group (NH2) [43–45]. The one small peak that appears at 3372 cm−1 is ascribed to the OH stretching frequency of carboxylic acid associated with the intramolecular hydrogen bonding to the amino group [43,45–47]. The two medium signals that appear at 1675 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 are ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of (CO) the carboxylate group (COOH), respectively [35,43,44]. Moreover, the stretching vibrational frequency at 1488 cm−1 is ascribed to the CC of the aromatic ring and a little peak that appears at 1246 cm−1, which gives information about the CN stretching vibration [35,44].

Similarly, in the spectrum of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3], the two absorption bands appear at 3470 cm−1 and 3324 cm−1, which ascribes the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of the amino group (NH2), respectively [35,44]. A strong peak at 3421 cm−1 due to the OH bond specifies the presence of coordinated water molecules that are coordinated with central metal in the complex [40,45,46]. In the spectrum, the CO stretching vibrational frequency of COOH at 1675 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 disappears completely. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching frequencies of the carboxylate group (COO−) shifted toward the lower wavenumber at 1620 cm−1 and 1506 cm−1, respectively, which support the complexation with Nd 3+ occur [35,45,46,48].

The morphology of the as-synthesized [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex was studied via SEM. Fig. 1 (c & d) represents the SEM image of the synthesized complex at 1 μm scale and 200 nm scale magnifications, respectively. The SEM images show the typical particle with a rod-shaped morphology. The elemental composition of [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] has been studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Fig. 2 (a) shows the corresponding image investigated for EDS spectroscopy. Fig. 2 (b) represents the EDS spectrum of complex indicating the existence of elements Nd, C, N, and O. Further, the elemental distribution was also investigated by the elemental mapping study as shown in Fig. 2 (c–f). The elemental mapping shows the uniform distribution of all the elements Nd, C, N, and O on the surface of the complex.

XPS was used to differentiate and identify the chemical structure and bonding state of all presented elements in the [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex. It also gives information about the oxidation state of central metal ion (Nd3+) in its complex [4,49,50]. First, the survey spectrum detected the existence of all available atoms (as shown in Fig. 3 a). At that time, the equivalent peaks were deconvoluted to identify the bonding positions of the disturbed atoms. The survey spectrum was estimated for the presence of C1s (69.91 %), O1s (21.25 %), N1s (5.33 %), and Nd3d (3.51 %). The Nd 3d spectrum of the [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex splits into two components of peaks at 982.56 eV for 3d5/2 and 1005.29 eV for 3d3/2 assigned respectively due to spin–orbit coupling with an energy variance of 23.27 eV [51,52,53]. The Nd 3d5/2 peak further deconvoluted into two specific peaks with binding energy at 982.48 eV and 980.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b) [49,51]. The higher intense peak (982.48 eV) was recognized as an NdO bond toward the higher energy side, and the lower intense peak (980.3 eV) appeared as a satellite peak towards the lower energy side, was ascribed to the transfer of electrons from the oxygen to Nd 4f3 orbital [51,54]. Similarly, the N-atoms of the complex give the N1s spectrum (see Fig. 3c), which is deconvoluted into two individual peaks at 398.82 eV and 399.44 eV, and they correspond to CN and NH bonding, respectively [55,56].

The C1s peak was deconvoluted into four different peaks at 284.18 eV, 284.59 eV, 285.58 eV, and 288.28 eV that are assigned to CC, CC, CN, and CO/CO bonding groups, respectively (Fig. 3d) [55,57]. And the O1s were deconvoluted into three key peaks (Fig. 3 e) located at 530.48 eV for NdO [52], 531.3 eV for associated water molecules [46,58,59], and 532.07 eV for the CO/CO bonding state, respectively [49].

The photoluminescent rare earth metal–organic frameworks having π-rich conjugated ligands are reported to be a key constituent for the increase in emission properties of the coordination complex due to charge transfer towards the metal by ligands (M ← L) [37,38,60]. The conjugated ligands act as antennas (photon energy absorbers) for rare earth metal nodes [37,38]. Nowadays, fluorescent sensors are broadly used for detecting metal ions and several kinds of drugs or biomolecules due to their non-destructive properties, high specificity, ease of operation, immunity to light scattering, and high sensitivity [46,61]. Taking the benefit of fluorescent enhancing characters (off-to-on manner) or quenching characters (on-to-off manner), the trace amount of ions, biomolecules, or drugs can be detected. Herein, we have synthesized the metal–ligand coordination [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex to detect trace amount of 6MP. The excitation spectrum of the coordination complex was noted in the excitation range of 200–380 nm, keeping the emission wavelength fixed at 403 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). There are two excitation spectra observed for the complex; the more intense peak appeared at a higher wavelength, 354 nm, and the lower intense peak appeared at 270 nm. Similarly, the emission spectrum was also noted in the emission range of 360–700 nm at a fixed excitation wavelength of 354 nm, expressed in Fig. 3b. The emission peak appeared at 403 nm may be attributed to the 4F3/2 → 4I11/2 transition [29,31,32,37].

Further, when 6MP was added to an aqueous FeCl3 solution, it gave rise to the formation of a coordinated mixed complex (Fe-6MP), as shown in Fig. S1 [62]. The emission responses of [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] is boosted by the addition of Fe-6MP into the testing medium. The emission responses of [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex, Fe-6MP, and [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] in the presence of Fe-6MP were recorded at
λ
ex
= 354 nm and pH = 7.4, as shown in Fig. 4 c. It was detected that, under the same environments, [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex showed a strong emission intensity at 403 nm due to the 4F3/2 → 4I11/2 transition [29,31,32,37], while the Fe-6MP exhibited very poor emission spectra in comparison to [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3].

To investigate this enhancement in emission intensity, we studied the as-synthesized complex and mixed two different amounts of Fe-6MP within it through time-resolved photoluminescence spectra. After exciting the samples with a green laser, we recorded the fluorescence intensity profile with time keeping all the measurement parameters the same for all three samples. The decay profile has been fitted with a single exponential decay function,
y t

y 0
+ a
e
- t / τ
, where
τ
corresponds to the excited state lifetime of the as-synthesized complex and
y 0
and
a
are fitting constants, all the parameters are tabulated in Table 1 .

From the perusal of Table 1, it has been found that the average lifetime of the excited states for [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex gets increased in the presence of Fe-6MP. That may be the cause of enhancement in fluorescence emission intensity of [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex. But, to understand why the average lifetime gets extended in the presence of Fe-6MP, we measured the UV–vis spectra of the as-synthesized complex and mixed the complex with Fe-6MP in a liquid medium (the same medium that was used for sensing application). As shown in Fig. 5 b, the normalized absorption maxima for [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex is found to be shifted about 9 nm towards the higher wavelength region in the presence of a very small amount of Fe-6MP. That confirms the interaction between Fe-6MP and the synthesized complex in the sensing medium. Further, we have calculated the Urbach energy (
E U
) for both cases from the low energy absorption tail. In organic materials, exponential tailing of the absorption edge at the lower energy side is mainly caused by the exciton scattering from π-π stacking disorders. Thus, the lower value of
E U
represents fewer lattice defects and a higher probability of molecular ordering through π-π interaction [63,64]. The Urbach energy has been evaluated from the relation,
l n
( α )
= l n
α 0
+
h ν
E U
; Here
α 0
is a constant,
h ν
and
α
are the absorption energy and absorbance, respectively and from the slope of
ln
α
v s h ν
linear plot, as shown in the Fig. 5c,
E U
has been estimated. It has been found that the value of Urbach energy is reduced from 103 meV to 93.7 meV in the presence of Fe-6MP. This can be explained as the presence of Fe-6MP with [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex favors the intermolecular π-π interaction, and thus a more ordered structure, which causes less Urbach energy. J. Ren et al. [65] showed from their experimental and theoretical results that an efficient intermolecular π-π interaction would cause a long persistent fluorescence lifetime. So, the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3], by the addition of Fe-6MP, may be due to the efficient π–π stacking interaction and intermolecular hydrogen bonding among the two complexes [66,67]. This experimental outcome proved that the [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex has the capability to interact with Fe-6MP drug. Employing this turn-on fluorescence response, we have developed a sensor to quantify the 6MP.

The emission spectra and solution-phase pH optimization are tailored for the detecting probe. The excitation spectrum suggests that energy levels situated at 354 nm (4 D 3/2 + 4 D 5/2 + 4 D 1/2) [41] are the more populated ones during the photoexcitation. The sensing probe, [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] complex should give dominant emission upon excited by 354 nm, and thus, we have chosen 354 nm as our excitation wavelength,
λ
ex
, for all the sensing measurements thereafter. Likewise, the pH of the sensor [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] is optimized by changing the pH value from 5.5 to 9.5. The decrease in fluorescent emission intensity is evident in Fig. 4d as the pH of the buffer solution increases from 5.5 to 9.5. However, the pH of the physiological biological system is 7.4, and the probe shows considerable fluorescent activity at this pH. Hence, a meticulous selection of pH 7.4 was made to detect 6MP molecules at the same excitation wavelength (354 nm).

The fluorescence sensing of 6MP was performed by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the probe [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3] at an excitation wavelength of 354 nm in the presence of different concentrations of iron coordinated 6MP starting from 2.57 μM to 45.51 μM in 2 ml (concentration: 50 μg/ml) [Nd (Anth)3. (H2O)3], as shown in Fig. 6 a. We observed a linear increase in the fluorescence emission intensity of the probe as well, with the increase in concentration of Fe-6MP. Fig. 6b shows the calibration plot between (I/I0) vs. concentration of Fe-6MP and displays the fluorescent enhancement in terms of I/I0 with Fe-6MP concentration, where I0 and I represent the fluorescent intensity in the absence and in the presence of Fe-6MP, respectively. This plot shows a linear range from 2.57 to 43.72 μM equivalent to the linear regression equation I/I0 = 1.02802–0.01996 [6MP], having a correlation coefficient of 0.99.
The LOD of the proposed sensor was found to be 0.26 μM and calculated using the formula LOD = (3 * standard deviation of the blank)/slope of the calibration plot.

To assess the selectivity of the developed fluorescence sensor, we have performed the measurement of fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of several possible interferences like L-cystine (cys), ascorbic acid (AA), L-glutathione (GSH), and bovine serum albumin (BSA), glycine (Gly), uric acid (UA), lysine (Lys), creatine (Cre), Cl−, PO4 2−, SO4 2−, Na+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, D-glucose (Glu) and D-galactose (Gal). The concentration of the interfering agents was taken ten times the concentration of 6MP, and the experiments were performed in similar conditions. The method deliberately maintained the respective error within a ±5 % margin while holding the 6MP concentration constant at 45 μM. As shown in Fig. 7 , among the all-interfering agents, only GHS, AA, and Cys insignificantly increase the intensity of the [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex while the remaining other interfering agents are least interfering with detecting 6MP. The result clearly signifies the selectivity of the developed sensor and supports its real-time application.

In order to further estimate the reliability of the sample, the developed sensor was useful for the sample analysis in the tablet samples. For the preparation of the tablet samples, a tablet was weighed and powdered in a pestle mortar for homogeneity. Further, the whole sample was made soluble in an aqueous solution by sonicating it for 4 h. The solution was again centrifuged and used to spike the samples needed to apply for the recovery analysis. Three concentrations were spiked i.e., 15, 26, 36 μM, and its response was recorded as shown in Table 2 . The analysis reported excellent recovery from 93 to 107 %, which validates the good performance of the developed sensor.

The performance of the fabricated fluorescence probe has been estimated and compared with the results of the previously reported procedures for detecting 6MP. Table 3 clearly shows the significance of our work, demonstrating the long linear range, better LOD, and good reproducibility. The proposed sensing probe provides an economical method of detecting 6MP without the involvement of any sophisticated instrumentations. The synthesized complex bears the advantage of facile one-step synthesis and room-temperature stability for its storage.

Herein, we have successfully synthesized the [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex with the help of Anth and Nd (III) nitrate hexahydrate, and its synthesis, structure, and morphology were validated by FT-IR spectroscopy, HRMS, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Nd3+ ion is surrounded by a bidentate Anth ligand and three labile water molecules in [Nd (Anth)3 (H2O)3] complex. The synthesized Nd (III) complex was found to possess excellent fluorescence properties, which further get enhanced with the addition of 6MP in the form of a [Fe (6MP)3]2+ mixed complex (Fe-6MP), which is formed by dissolving it in FeCl3. Employing this enhancement in the signal, which may be due to the π–π stacking interaction and formation of hydrogen bonds of the ligand with the Fe-6MP, a fluorescence sensor was successfully developed. The fabricated sensor displayed a good linear response in a wide concentration range from 2.55 μM to 45.51 μM with a detection limit of 0.26 μM at physiological pH.
